roots.title: Gradient Descent, Midterm Logistics, and Feature Engineering
roots.summary: Gradient descent, especially stochastic gradient descent, is essential in AI for model selection and minimizing loss functions like Mean Squared Error. The midterm is on October 23 from 7-9 PM, with a review session on October 21 from 1:30-3:00 PM in Evans 10. The review covers the St. Caster Gray algorithm, gradient descent techniques, the importance of data shuffling, and feature engineering, including transformations and one-hot encoding to improve model performance and interpretability.

children.title: Gradient Descent Overview and Midterm Logistics
children.summary: Gradient descent, particularly stochastic gradient descent, is crucial in AI, involving model selection, defining a loss function like Mean Squared Error, and minimizing average loss through iterative adjustments. The midterm is scheduled for October 23 from 7-9 PM, with a review session on October 21 from 1:30-3:00 PM in Evans 10, and further details on seating and accommodations are provided in relevant posts.

children.title: Gradient Descent Overview and Midterm Logistics
children.summary: The focus is on gradient descent, especially stochastic gradient descent, an essential algorithm in AI. The midterm is on October 23rd from 7 to 9 PM, with a review session on Friday in Evans 10 from 1:30 to 3:00 PM, accommodating approximately 230 students. Further midterm details, including seating assignments and accommodations, are available in the relevant posts.

children.title: Gradient Descent Overview and Midterm Logistics
children.summary: The discussion will focus on gradient descent, particularly stochastic gradient descent, a key algorithm in AI. The midterm is scheduled for October 23rd from 7 to 9 PM, with a review session on Friday in Evans 10 from 1:30 to 3:00 PM, offering first-come, first-serve seating for about 230 students. Additional midterm details, including seating assignments and accommodations, can be found in the relevant posts.

children.title: Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Overview
children.summary: The lecture will continue the discussion on gradient descent, focusing on completing the previous session's material. There is an intention to cover stochastic gradient descent, which is a significant algorithm in contemporary AI.

children.title: Midterm Logistics and Review Session Details
children.summary: The midterm is scheduled for Wednesday, October 23 from 7-9 PM. A review session will take place on Friday in Evans 10 from 1:30-3:00 PM, with seating on a first-come, first-serve basis for approximately 230 seats. Recordings and slides will be posted on Ed after the session, and seating assignments for the in-person midterm will be sent out on Monday, October 21. Students needing accommodations should fill out the designated form.

children.title: Midterm Details and Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: The midterm is scheduled for October 23 from 7-9 PM, with a review session on October 21 from 1:30-3:00 PM in Evans 10. Gradient descent involves selecting a model, defining a loss function (Mean Squared Error), and minimizing average loss through iterative parameter adjustments. The discussion includes high-dimensional models, the optimization of multiple parameters, and the significance of visualizing loss surfaces, along with the role of gradient vectors in determining the steepest descent direction for effective optimization.

children.title: Midterm Schedule and Review Session Details
children.summary: The midterm is on Wednesday, October 23 from 7-9 PM, with a review session on Friday in Evans 10 from 1:30-3:00 PM on a first-come, first-serve basis. Seating assignments will be provided on Monday, 10/21, and recordings and slides will be available on Ed afterwards. DSP students will receive separate emails regarding accommodations.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Overview, Steps, and MSE
children.summary: The gradient descent process involves selecting a model, defining a loss function (Mean Squared Error), and minimizing average loss. It includes computing the gradient of the loss and iteratively adjusting model parameters in the opposite direction of the gradient. The algorithm starts with an initial guess and updates it until the gradient is zero, indicating the minimum point has been reached.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Overview and Steps
children.summary: The lecture discusses the process of modeling using gradient descent, emphasizing the steps involved: choosing a model, selecting a loss function, and minimizing the average loss. It highlights the Mean Squared Error (MSE) as the loss function and outlines the iterative update formula for fitting the model using gradient descent.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Process and Model Fitting
children.summary: The iterative algorithm of gradient descent involves selecting a model, defining a loss function (specifically Mean Squared Error), and minimizing the average loss. The process includes computing the gradient of the loss, adjusting the model parameters in the opposite direction of the gradient, and iteratively refining the model to achieve lower loss.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Algorithm and MSE Explanation
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm starts with an initial guess and iteratively updates this guess by subtracting a scaled gradient. The mean squared error (MSE) is used to evaluate the model's performance, and the goal is to minimize this error by adjusting the model parameters. The process continues until the gradient is zero, indicating that the minimum point has been reached.

children.title: High-dimensional models and gradient descent optimization
children.summary: Models can have one or more parameters, with simple linear regression having one and multiple linear regression involving several. In advanced applications, models can reach dimensions from 10 to 400 billion. The extension of gradient descent for optimizing multiple parameters is introduced, with formulas for simple and multiple linear regression, highlighting the importance of visualizing a loss surface to assess model loss across parameter combinations.

children.title: High-dimensional models: parameters and optimization
children.summary: Models can have one or more parameters; for instance, simple linear regression has one parameter, while multiple linear regression involves multiple parameters. In modern applications, models can reach very high dimensions, with examples ranging from 10 to 10,000 or even 400 billion dimensions in advanced AI contexts.

children.title: Extending Gradient Descent to Multiple Dimensions
children.summary: The concept of extending gradient descent to optimize multiple parameters in models is introduced. In simple linear regression, the formula is presented as ŷ = β0 + β1X, while multiple linear regression is expressed as ŷ = β0 + β1X1 + β2X2 + ... + βpXp. The discussion emphasizes the need to visualize a loss surface to evaluate the model's loss for different parameter combinations.

children.title: Optimization: Loss Surface and Gradient Vectors
children.summary: The segment covers optimization in learning algorithms, focusing on minimizing loss curves through the loss surface in multiple dimensions. The gradient vector, derived from the loss function's partial derivatives, indicates the steepest descent direction for effective optimization. It includes a discussion on calculating gradients for a specific 2D function, emphasizing the importance of understanding partial derivatives in this context.

children.title: Optimization: Loss Surface and Gradient Vector
children.summary: The focus is on optimization in learning algorithms, specifically reaching the minimum of a loss curve. A loss surface is considered in 2D or higher dimensions to evaluate the model's loss for various parameter combinations. The derivative of the loss function indicates the direction of steepest descent, represented by the gradient vector, guiding towards the minimum.

children.title: Understanding Gradient Vectors in Loss Minimization
children.summary: The derivative of the loss function indicates the optimal direction towards its minimum on a 2D surface, represented by a vector. To compute the gradient, take the partial derivative of the loss with respect to each parameter, which helps identify the negative gradient direction for effective optimization.

children.title: Interpreting Gradients and Loss Changes
children.summary: The segment discusses how to interpret gradients in relation to model weights and loss. It explains that nudging model weights affects the loss, with the gradient indicating the direction to adjust parameters to minimize loss. The gradient vector, derived from the partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter, always points downhill on the loss surface.

children.title: Gradient Descent: 1D to 2D Update Rules
children.summary: The segment explains the transition from the 1D update rule to the 2D update rule in gradient descent. It discusses taking the partial derivatives of the loss function with respect to each parameter and how to update guesses iteratively using vectors. The gradient vector indicates the direction of steepest descent on the loss surface, and the update formula is adapted for multiple parameters.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Multi-Dimensional Update Rule
children.summary: The loss function is typically optimized as an average in modeling tasks. For multiple parameters, the update rule for gradient descent is expressed in vector form, nudging parameters in the direction of the negative gradient until convergence is achieved. The next value for the parameters is calculated using the gradient of the loss function evaluated at the current parameters.

children.title: Gradient Descent Update Rule and Model Dimensions

children.title: Gradient Descent: Mean Squared Loss and Updates

children.title: Gradient Descent Stopping Conditions
children.summary: Two common stopping conditions for gradient descent are: running a fixed number of iterations, such as 10,000, and stopping when the updates to the parameters become negligible, indicating the algorithm is close to convergence. The process involves nudging parameters in the negative gradient direction until they settle on a solution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'll see you next time. Okay. One thing we spent a little time on last week, we probably have kind of finally some more. When do I stop running this iterative procedure? It's actually an art. Uh, one. Very common strategy is... 10,000 times. You pick a number that you're willing to wait that long. You run at that many steps. More awesome! Equally often we'll pick A measure of theta. just not changing much anymore, you're taking really really small steps because the gradients are getting really really small because you're getting really close to the bottom of that curve. It's pretty flat. You might stop then too. There's two stopping conditions for gradient descent. Video: 1: Gradient Descent Update Rule

children.title: Gradient Descent: Update Rule and Multi-Dimensional Models
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm updates parameters in the negative gradient direction until convergence is achieved, defined as minimal changes in parameters or reaching a fixed number of updates. In a multi-dimensional context, the model predicts tips based on total bill and a constant offset, requiring optimization of two parameters. The mean squared error (MSE) is minimized by incorporating a bias term into the data.

Notebook [7
Gradient Descent on Multi-Dimensional Models
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Now suppose we improve our model so that we want to predict the tip from the total_bill plus a constant offset, in other words:
tip = 00+01bill
Our simple linear regression model has two parameters, 00 and 01. We need to optimize both of them.
Fortunately, gradient descent readily extends to models with multiple dimenions.
Defining a 2D MSE Function
Now, we can give our predictions as
§ = 00 + 01x1
In the cell below, we add a bias term to our data to represent the constant intercept 00.
[ ]:
tips_with_bias = df.copy( )
tips_with_bias ["bias"] = 1
tips_with_bias = tips_with_bias [ ["bias", "total_bill"] ]
tips_with_bias.head( )
[ ]:
X = tips_with_bias
y = df ["tip"]
Throughout this problem, we'll assume we want to minimize the mean squared error of our predictions, i.e.
[ ]: def mse_loss (theta):
y_hat = X @ theta
return np.mean((y - y_hat) ** 2)
Using this function, we can visualize our loss function. Because we now want to understand how the loss changes with respect to two parameters, we create a loss surface. Each point on the surface
represents the loss of the model for a particular choice of 00 and 01.
The cell below uses lots of syntax you've never seen. No need to worry about any unfamiliar plotting code; for now, focus on the output.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Linear Regression Optimization
children.summary: The gradient descent algorithm is discussed for optimizing parameters in a simple linear regression model, focusing on the constant intercept and slope term. It details the iterative parameter updates aimed at minimizing the mean squared error (MSE) of predictions and introduces the concept of a loss surface to visualize changes in loss with respect to the parameters.

children.title: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression Optimization
children.summary: The segment discusses the gradient descent algorithm for optimizing parameters in a simple linear regression model, which includes a constant intercept and a slope term. It explains how to update parameters iteratively until convergence, minimizing the mean squared error (MSE) of predictions. Additionally, it introduces the concept of a loss surface to visualize how the loss changes with respect to the two parameters.

children.title: Linear Regression: Loss Function and Parameters
children.summary: The segment covers a simple linear regression model using the tips dataset, highlighting two parameters: theta zero (intercept) and theta one (slope). It emphasizes minimizing the loss function, represented by the mean squared error of predictions, and uses visualizations to show how different model choices impact the loss surface and the optimization process for both parameters.

children.title: Linear Regression: Loss Function and Parameters
children.summary: The discussion focuses on a simple linear regression model using the tips dataset, featuring two parameters: theta zero (intercept) and theta one (slope). The goal is to minimize the loss function, which corresponds to the mean squared error of predictions. Visualizations illustrate how different model choices affect the loss on a surface, emphasizing the optimization process for both parameters.

Using this function, we can visualize our loss function. Because we now want to understand how the loss changes with respect to two parameters, we create a loss surface. Each point on the surface

The cell below uses lots of syntax you've never seen. No need to worry about any unfamiliar plotting code; for now, focus on the output.
[ ]:
import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

Throughout this problem, we'll assume we want to minimize the mean squared error of our predictions, i.e.
[49]:
def mse_loss (theta):
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
y_hat = X @ theta
return np.mean((y - y_hat) ** 2)
Using this function, we can visualize our loss function. Because we now want to understand how the loss changes with respect to two parameters, we create a loss surface. Each point on the surface
represents the loss of the model for a particular choice of 00 and 01.
The cell below uses lots of syntax you've never seen. No need to worry about any unfamiliar plotting code; for now, focus on the output.
[ ]:
import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

import plotly. graph_objects as go
uvalues = np. linspace(-1, 5, 20)
vvalues = np. linspace(-0.1, 0.35, 20)
(u,v) = np.meshgrid(uvalues, vvalues)
thetas = np.vstack((u.flatten( ), v. flatten()))
MSE = np.array( [mse_loss(t) for t in thetas.T] )
loss_surface = go. Surface (x=u,
y=v, z=np. reshape (MSE, u. shape),
contours = {"z": {"show": True, "start": 0, "end": 50, "size": 2, "color": "white"}} )
# This is an approximate guess for the optimal point.
ind = np.argmin (MSE)
optimal_point = go. Scatter3d(name = "Optimal Point",
x = [thetas. T [ind, 0]], y = [thetas. T [ind, 1] ] ,
z = [MSE [ind] ] ,
marker=dict(size=10, color="red") )
fig = go. Figure(data=[loss_surface, optimal_point] )
fig. update_layout (scene = dict(
xaxis_title = "theta0",
yaxis_title = "theta1",
zaxis_title = "MSE"), autosize=False, width=800, height=600)
fig. show( )

children.title: Visualizing Loss and Gradient Descent Optimization
children.summary: Heat maps and contour plots are used to visualize loss, with brighter colors indicating higher loss and darker colors indicating lower loss. In high-dimensional data, contour plots help visualize the loss surface. The update rule for gradient descent involves optimizing a vector of parameters using the gradient of the loss function derived from mean squared error, with final theta values including an initial bill amount and a learned tip percentage.

children.title: Visualizing Loss with Heat Maps and Contour Plots
children.summary: Heat maps visualize loss contours, with brighter colors indicating higher loss and darker colors indicating lower loss. In high-dimensional data, visualizing loss becomes challenging, but contour plots can provide a bird's-eye view of the loss surface. The update rule for applying gradient descent in multidimensional models involves optimizing a vector of parameters using the gradient of the loss function, derived from the mean squared error.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Parameter Optimization and Loss Derivation
children.summary: The gradients for the loss function have been derived, with a focus on optimizing model parameters using gradient descent. The update rule for parameters is introduced, and the final theta values for the model are presented, including an initial bill amount and a learned tip percentage.

With a small learning rate starting at initial guess, and here all my theaters. We're on the same optimization using, uh, actually, let's start by this first. So here is the final theta that I got for adding that algorithm. This would be my choice of model parameters. My initial bill amount and the 10.1 percent tip that I'd learned from this model. def mse_gradient (theta):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(32 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)))
dth1 = np.mean(-2* (y- (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.
The cell below may take a moment to run.
[ ]:
guesses = gradient_descent (mse_gradient, np.array( [1, . 5]), 0.001, 10000)
pd. DataFrame (guesses, columns=["theta_0", "theta_1"] ).tail (10)

We can actually provide the gradient information to the scipy optimizer to get an even faster solution.

dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0] ++ theta[1]*x1)) * x1)

When working with multidimensional models, we optimize a vector of parameters. Our new update rule is:
@(+1) = 0(+) - aV;L(@(*)
Where V -L(0(t)) is the gradient of the loss function. It is the vector of the partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter 0 ;.
Let's derive the gradient of the loss for our problem:

def mse_gradient (theta):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(32 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)))
dth1 = np.mean(-2* (y- (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
Now, we can use our gradient_descent function from before to optimize 00 and 01 at the same time! The final estimates for the ideal model parameters are very similar to the guesses we may have made
simply by inspecting the plot of the loss surface from before.
The cell below may take a moment to run.
[ ]:

We can actually provide the gradient information to the scipy optimizer to get an even faster solution.
[ ]: minimize(mse_loss, x0 = [0,0],jac=mse_gradient)

Where V -L(0(t)) is the gradient of the loss function. It is the vector of the partial derivatives of loss with respect to each parameter 0 ;.

children.title: Gradient Optimization and Complexity Analysis
children.summary: Gradient optimization using the Minimize package is essential for model efficiency, leveraging gradient estimates for faster convergence in large datasets. Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is introduced as a key algorithm for 2024, particularly in multi-dimensional models. The computational complexity of gradient descent is analyzed, revealing an overall complexity of O(Tnd) after T iterations, emphasizing the significant workload involved with large n and d values, and prompting discussions on potential cost-reduction techniques.

children.title: Gradient optimization using Minimize package
children.summary: The gradient with respect to vector theta is crucial for model optimization. The Minimize package estimates gradients through data sampling, facilitating gradient descent with fewer iterations, which is beneficial for large datasets. Understanding parameters like alpha and convergence behavior is essential for effective implementation of optimization routines.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And again, all you need to do that was to define a gradient respect to my vector theta. for this month. And that is a really powerful tool. So you call it a crazy model as long as you can take those derivatives. You can optimize that model and fit it to your data. So last lecture, I talked about a package called Minimize. It's very sophisticated. It tries to estimate the gradient by sampling the data, sampling points on the surface, sort of like measuring the curving, like I'll take a few points here, maybe I'll put a plane to it and I get a guess what the gradient looks like. That function... Also has green descent built into it. So you can construct your gradient function. You can pass that in. I'll take far fewer iterations to solve the problem. Which is good because if this data was big this could take a long time. So this gives you another way to optimize. In practice, you'll probably do this, or you'll suffer packages that run gradient descent. The reason I'm telling you all of this stuff is that you'll need to set parameters like this alpha and you'd look at things like whether or not it's converging as you're writing that up from station routine. def mse_gradient (theta) :
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""

leaf_chunk.audio: And again, all you need to do that was to define a gradient respect to my vector theta. for this month. And that is a really powerful tool. So you call it a crazy model as long as you can take those derivatives. You can optimize that model and fit it to your data. So last lecture, I talked about a package called Minimize. It's very sophisticated. It tries to estimate the gradient by sampling the data, sampling points on the surface, sort of like measuring the curving, like I'll take a few points here, maybe I'll put a plane to it and I get a guess what the gradient looks like. That function... Also has green descent built into it. So you can construct your gradient function. You can pass that in. I'll take far fewer iterations to solve the problem. Which is good because if this data was big this could take a long time. So this gives you another way to optimize. In practice, you'll probably do this, or you'll suffer packages that run gradient descent. The reason I'm telling you all of this stuff is that you'll need to set parameters like this alpha and you'd look at things like whether or not it's converging as you're writing that up from station routine. And now you have an intuition of what's going on when we do that.

[53]:
def mse_gradient (theta) :
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
#x0 = X.iloc[ :, 0] constant term (1)
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0] + theta[1]*x1)) * 1)
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - (theta[0]+ theta[1]*x1)) * x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1] )

children.title: Introduction to Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is presented as an exciting algorithm for 2024, with a brief engagement with the audience regarding their prior knowledge of the topic. The segment transitions into discussing the implementation of SGD in multi-dimensional models, highlighting its importance in optimization.

[ ]: def mse_gradient (theta, X, y):
"""Returns the gradient of the MSE on our data for the given theta"""
x0 = X. iloc[ :, 0]
x1 = X. iloc[ :, 1]
dth0 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) * x0)
dth1 = np.mean(-2 * (y - theta[0]*x0 - theta[1]*x1) *x1)
return np. array ( [dth0, dth1])
[ ]: def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):
theta = initial_theta
thetas = [theta]
n = len (X)
for t in range(1, max_iter) :
X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas
thetas = sadimse gradient. X. v.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Gradient Approximation and Techniques
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) uses probability samples to approximate the true gradient by averaging over sampled records, enhancing computational efficiency. Increasing mini-batch size improves gradient estimation and reduces noise, allowing for better consistency in minimizing loss. The choice of mini-batch size influences both the effectiveness of gradient estimation and the ability to perform parallel computations.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Sampling the Gradient
children.summary: A probability sample can be used to approximate the gradient in stochastic gradient descent. By sampling B records from the data without replacement, the gradient can be computed as an average over these sampled data points, providing an efficient approximation.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Gradient Approximation
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) involves using a probability sample to approximate the true gradient. The true gradient is represented as the expected value of the function over all data points, while SGD samples a subset of records to estimate this gradient effectively.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Noise Reduction Techniques
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) involves taking random data points to estimate the direction for minimizing loss, but this can lead to noisy results. To reduce noise, increasing the batch size allows for a better estimate of the gradient, resulting in more consistent movement towards the minimum. This approach utilizes probability sampling to approximate the true gradient effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why don't we do? One. This was the original stochastic grain descent algorithm. Take one data point out of all of my data. Which way does that data point think I should move if I want to minimize the loss on just that data point? Take a tiny step in that direction. Take another random data point, you know what it says go backwards okay well that's a bummer i take another point Let's go this way a little bit On average, all these data points are going to make little steps wandering all over the surface, but eventually... Hopefully. They will take me to the bottom. But I just made the come. that costed during this much smaller. Right, but they're really noisy. How could I make it less noisy? As I make my B will eventually be called batch size bigger. I'm going to get a better estimate of the curvature of the direction of the descent in my surface. children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Mini-batch Size Impact
children.summary: The choice of mini-batch size (b) in Stochastic Gradient Descent affects gradient estimation and computational efficiency. A larger b provides a better gradient estimate and allows for parallel computation across multiple GPUs. Typically, mini-batch sizes are small, with the original algorithm using b=1, while current practices vary based on several factors.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, my choice would be actually ends up depending on several factors. Oh no. In practice, we're going to use a slightly larger B to get a better gradient estimate. children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Gradient Sampling Techniques
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) utilizes probability samples to approximate the gradient, allowing for better estimates through mini-batch sampling. The mini-batch size, typically small, can influence the effectiveness of the gradient estimation and parallelism in computations. Larger mini-batch sizes can improve gradient estimates, but they also exhibit rapidly diminishing returns.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) estimates the true gradient of the loss surface using a sample of data, allowing for quicker gradient computation. The process involves running K epochs over the dataset, shuffling the data to maintain the relationship between X and Y, and updating the theta parameter using mini-batches.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Mini-batch Approach
children.summary: Stochastic gradient descent uses a mini-batch of data to compute the gradient, allowing for faster updates than using the entire dataset. By selecting a batch size, such as 10, the algorithm can take more steps with smaller adjustments to avoid overshooting. Shuffling the data ensures that each step is based on a random sample, enhancing the stochastic nature of the process.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Data Shuffling and Efficiency
children.summary: Shuffling data before processing can improve the efficiency of Stochastic Gradient Descent by allowing for linear traversal of data, which is faster than repeatedly sampling from large datasets. This method estimates the true gradient using mini-batches, enabling quicker gradient computation while handling computational challenges associated with large data.

children.title: Data Shuffling in Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: Shuffling data before processing is a common practice to ensure randomness in sampling, though some may choose not to shuffle if the data is in arbitrary order. While shuffling can mimic sampling with replacement, it is not strictly necessary for convergence and can be time-consuming. Generally, it is advisable to shuffle data at least once to avoid issues with ordered data.

children.title: Stochastic vs Batch Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) utilizes mini-batches for faster gradient computation by shuffling data and selecting batch sizes, contrasting with batch gradient descent that computes the true gradient. An example illustrates fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to 8,192 data points with SGD, prompting a poll on the number of gradient updates per epoch, highlighting that multiple updates occur in SGD compared to a single update in vanilla gradient descent.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Concepts and Comparisons
children.summary: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) uses mini-batches to estimate the true gradient of the loss surface, allowing for quicker gradient computation. The process involves shuffling the data and selecting a batch size for each gradient calculation. In comparing gradient descent techniques, batch gradient descent computes the true gradient and consistently descends towards the minimum, while mini-batch SGD approximates the gradient and may not always descend towards the true minimum.

children.title: Gradient Descent: Stochastic vs Batch
children.summary: The discussion contrasts batch gradient descent, which computes the true gradient, with stochastic gradient descent, which approximates the true gradient and may not consistently descend towards the true minimum of the loss. An example is provided involving fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to an 8192 data point dataset using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1, prompting a question about the number of gradient updates per epoch.

children.title: Ordinary Least Squares and Stochastic Gradient Descent
children.summary: Fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model to a dataset of 8,192 data points using stochastic gradient descent with a batch size of 1 raises the question of how many gradient updates occur per epoch. The correct answer is not one, as the context of stochastic gradient descent allows for multiple updates per epoch, contrary to vanilla gradient descent.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Overview
children.summary: Vanilla gradient descent uses all 8,192 data points to construct a gradient and take one careful step, while stochastic gradient descent updates the model using one data point at a time, repeating the process for all data points. The segment also includes an active poll regarding the number of gradient updates per epoch when fitting an Ordinary Least Squares model with a batch size of 1.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One gradient step, right? Vanilla gradient descent means I take all 8,192 data points. Construct the gradient. Really good gradient and I take one careful step. One great step. gradient descent says I take just one data point. Contract the curry. Take one. Half-hazard's the caustic step. And they take another point, take another half-hattered, construct the gradient, take another step. I'm not a new location. Take another data point. It says I should go backwards. Repeat this process 8,192 times. We're going to fix it. Video: 1: : Active poll

children.title: Epochs and Gradient Descent Steps
children.summary: An epoch represents one complete scan over the data, and the number of gradient descent steps taken depends on the batch size. If the batch size equals the data size, only one gradient step occurs per epoch. However, with a batch size of one, multiple gradient updates are computed during each epoch.

children.title: Training epochs and batch sizes explained
children.summary: More than one epoch is used in training models like ChatGPT due to the vast amount of data, such as trillions of data points, compared to the 10,000 data points in this class. Each epoch involves taking multiple steps, and batch sizes affect gradient computation; for example, using a batch size of 1 means computing the gradient on each data point individually before averaging.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Gradient Computation
children.summary: The process involves taking two data points to compute the gradient, averaging those gradients to approximate the true gradient at the current position, and then taking another step. This iterative procedure is a key aspect of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I have 8,092 minus 2 or 8,190. Data points left. I'll take two more. Construct the gradient for each of those averages. Gradients that will give me the gradient I'm going to take where I'm standing now. I'll take another step. And I'll grab two more data points. Compute the gradient on those data points. You'll average those gradients to get the gradient at this point for two data points, an approximation of the true gradient. And it says go this way. I'll take another step. That's the procedure. children.title: Batch Size Impact on Gradient Updates
children.summary: Smaller batch sizes lead to more frequent gradient updates with higher variability, while larger batch sizes provide more accurate gradient estimates but less frequent updates. Choosing an appropriate batch size is crucial for hardware efficiency, and many software packages will automatically select these parameters, though tuning may be necessary.

children.title: Batch Size Tradeoff and SGD in PyTorch
children.summary: Smaller batch sizes provide more frequent gradient updates but with higher variability, while larger batch sizes leverage hardware acceleration for better performance and higher quality gradient updates, though they result in less frequent updates. The typical solution is to set the batch size to optimize hardware utilization. Additionally, a simple SGD function in PyTorch is outlined, emphasizing the use of software packages to compute gradients automatically.

children.title: SGD Implementation in PyTorch Overview
children.summary: The segment introduces a simple implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in PyTorch, highlighting its utility for data scientists, even though it is out of scope for Data 100. It outlines the function definition including parameters like model, loss function, dataset, learning rate, number of epochs, and batch size. Key steps in the SGD process are discussed, including loading the dataset, computing loss, using software packages for gradient computation, and updating model parameters.

children.title: Gradient Descent Implementation in Python
children.summary: The segment discusses implementing gradient descent using a two-dimensional model with specific parameters such as initial theta, learning rate, maximum iterations, and batch size. It details a sampling strategy that ensures corresponding X and Y values are selected from the data frame, emphasizing the importance of maintaining the relationship between them.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent Implementation
children.summary: The learning rate is adjusted to prevent overshooting during gradient descent. A sample path visualization will be provided later, illustrating the movement through parameter space. The implementation includes a function for calculating the mean squared error gradient and a stochastic gradient descent function that samples data and updates parameters iteratively.

children.title: Midterm Review: Neural Networks and Feature Engineering
children.summary: The midterm review emphasizes the significance of assignments and the St. Caster Gray algorithm for training large neural networks and generative AI. It covers gradient descent techniques, distinguishing between epochs and stochastic gradient descent while highlighting the importance of data shuffling to prevent bias. Additionally, feature engineering is discussed as a vital process for transforming data to create linear relationships in models, utilizing techniques such as logarithmic and polynomial transformations to improve model performance.

children.title: Midterm Review and Feature Engineering Techniques
children.summary: The midterm content has been summarized, highlighting the importance of reviewing assignments and the St. Caster Gray algorithm for training large neural networks and generative AI. Gradient descent techniques are discussed, including the distinction between epochs and stochastic gradient descent, emphasizing data shuffling to avoid bias. Feature engineering is explored as a crucial process for transforming data to establish linear relationships in models, utilizing techniques like logarithmic and polynomial transformations, and enhancing model performance by converting raw features into more informative ones.

children.title: Midterm content summary and St. Caster Gray algorithm
children.summary: The material needed for the midterm has been concluded, with an emphasis on reviewing assignments. The St. Caster Gray algorithm is noted as a key algorithm for 2024, essential for training large neural networks and generative AI, and it utilizes the previously demonstrated code.

children.title: Midterm content summary and algorithm highlight
children.summary: This concludes the material needed for the midterm, with a reminder to review assignments. The St. Caster Gray algorithm is highlighted as a leading algorithm for 2024, integral to training large neural networks and generative AI, utilizing the previously shown code.

children.title: Gradient Descent Techniques and Feature Engineering
children.summary: A break is announced before discussing feature engineering. The concept of epochs in gradient descent is clarified, highlighting the use of all data points for a single gradient computation. Stochastic gradient descent is explained as taking samples to compute gradients iteratively, with a focus on the relationship between steps and batch size. The importance of shuffling data to prevent bias in sampling is emphasized, using a card shuffling analogy, and the iterative nature of fitting models through gradient descent is discussed.

children.title: Stochastic Gradient Descent: Step Computation
children.summary: Stochastic gradient descent involves taking a sample from the data, computing the gradient, and then taking a step to a new state. The process is repeated with another block of data to compute the gradient at the new location. The discussion includes how many steps are taken in relation to the overall dataset.

children.title: Importance of Shuffling in Sampling Techniques
children.summary: Shuffling data is crucial to eliminate biases when sampling, as it ensures that similar data points are not clustered together, which can hinder the effectiveness of stochastic gradient descent. While shuffling can be expensive, it may be done less frequently in practice, maintaining a semblance of randomness. The discussion uses the analogy of shuffling a deck of cards to illustrate the concept of avoiding biases in data arrangement.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: No, you popped my data. Now, I keep going to data in the same order I should maybe shuffle it because I am trying to sample because we know that sampling helps us eliminate these biases. Get this simple random samples and idea where to go. So I should probably shuffle all the data every time I do this. That's expensive and in practice I might shuffle it once and walk through it. I might shuffle it again. Pretty lazy and it's expensive and it was a good shuffle the first time I'll walk through the deck again. So, let me take a deck of cards and shuffle it. You walk through it you walk through it again it still feels kind of like random deck it's sort of the same random deck is last time but the biases of having like all the the hearts beside each other aren't there. And those have been the biases we'd have a problem with when doing stochastic brain descent. If all the things that are the same or beside each other and they cause me to just run backwards and then all the other things that are the same cause we run forwards, I just get stuck in this kind of forward backwards loop. children.title: Feature Engineering for Linear Models
children.summary: Feature engineering transforms data to establish linear relationships for linear models, including neural networks. Techniques like logarithmic and polynomial transformations are employed to linearize datasets, facilitating effective optimization with linear modeling methods.

children.title: Feature Engineering: Transforming Data for Linear Models

Techniques such as applying logarithmic or polynomial transformations can help linearize datasets, allowing for effective optimization using linear modeling methods.

children.title: Feature Engineering: Transforming Features for Models
children.summary: Feature engineering enhances model performance by transforming raw features, especially in linear models that require linear relationships. This process helps to linearize data and better model non-linear relationships, integrating domain knowledge to create new features that capture underlying patterns.

children.title: Feature Engineering: Transforming Features for Models
children.summary: Feature engineering involves transforming raw features to enhance model performance, particularly in linear models, which thrive on linear relationships in datasets. Transformations can help linearize data, allowing for better modeling of non-linear relationships. This process integrates domain knowledge to create new features that reflect underlying patterns.

children.title: Feature Engineering: Transforming Qualitative Data
children.summary: Feature engineering is the process of converting raw features into more informative ones for modeling, allowing for the capture of domain knowledge and non-linear relationships. Feature functions describe these transformations, often increasing dimensionality, such as by adding squared features to the design matrix.

children.title: Feature Engineering: Transforming Qualitative Data
children.summary: Feature engineering involves converting raw features into more informative features for modeling, enabling the capture of domain knowledge, expression of non-linear relationships, and incorporation of non-numeric features. A feature function describes the transformations applied to raw features, often increasing the dataset's dimensionality, such as adding squared features to the design matrix.

children.title: Feature Transformations and One-Hot Encoding Techniques
children.summary: Feature transformations apply nonlinear functions to raw features, enhancing dimensionality and encoding categorical data for regression. One-hot encoding converts qualitative features into numeric format by creating binary columns for each category, applicable for nominal and text data, thus aiding in modeling language through vocabulary encoding.

children.title: Feature Transformations and One-Hot Encoding Techniques
children.summary: Feature transformations involve applying nonlinear functions to raw features, which can increase dimensionality and help encode categorical data for regression. One-hot encoding is a method that converts qualitative features into numeric format by creating binary columns for each category, making it applicable for both nominal and text data, thus facilitating the modeling of language through vocabulary encoding.

children.title: Feature Transformations and One-Hot Encoding Techniques
children.summary: Feature transformations apply nonlinear functions to raw features, potentially increasing dimensionality, while addressing challenges in encoding categorical data for regression. One-hot encoding is introduced as a method to convert qualitative features into numeric format, creating binary columns for each category, thus allowing regression models to effectively utilize non-numeric data. This technique is suitable for both nominal data and text data, enabling the modeling of language through vocabulary encoding.

children.title: Feature Transformations and Encoding
children.summary: Feature transformations involve applying nonlinear functions to raw features, potentially increasing the dataset's dimensionality. Notation is introduced for transformed features, allowing for various mathematical operations. The discussion includes challenges in encoding categorical data, such as words, for regression analysis.

children.title: One-hot Encoding for Non-Numeric Features
children.summary: One-hot encoding is introduced as a technique to incorporate qualitative features into regression models by transforming them into numeric format. For example, in the tips dataset, categorical features like 'day of the week' can be converted into multiple binary columns, where each column represents a category and is marked with a '1' for the corresponding value and '0' otherwise. This allows the model to utilize non-numeric data effectively.

children.title: One-hot Encoding: Transforming Qualitative Data
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique that converts qualitative data into numeric features for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, with a value of 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise.

children.title: One-hot Encoding: Transforming Categorical Data
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a feature engineering technique that converts qualitative data into numeric features for modeling. Each category of a categorical variable is assigned its own feature, where the value is 1 if a row belongs to that category and 0 otherwise. This method avoids prioritizing any feature and is suitable for nominal data.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding: Transforming Qualitative Data
children.summary: One-hot encoding is a technique for converting qualitative data into numeric features for modeling, where each category of a categorical variable is represented by its own feature. A value of 1 indicates membership in a category, while 0 indicates absence. This method is effective for both categorical variables and text data, allowing for the modeling of language by encoding vocabulary. The one-hot encoded features can then be utilized in the design matrix for training models.

children.title: One-Hot Encoding for Regression Analysis
children.summary: One-hot encoding is necessary to convert categorical string data into numerical format for regression analysis. The segment demonstrates how to encode the 'day of the week' feature from the tips dataset and outlines the regression equation using one-hot encoded features. Additionally, a function for calculating the gradient of the mean squared error (MSE) and a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implementation are introduced.

Feature Engineering

One-hot encoding
· Polynomial features
To do so, we'll work with our familiar tips dataset.

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

def sgd(grad, X, y, initial_theta, eta = 0.3, max_iter = 5000, batch_size=50 ):
theta = initial_theta
thetas = [theta]
n = len (X)
for t in range(1, max_iter) :
X_sample = X.sample(batch_size)
y_sample = y. loc [X_sample. index]
theta = theta - eta/t * grad(theta, X_sample, y_sample)
thetas. append (theta)
return thetas

It turns out that the examples we looked at above represent a somewhat artificial scenario: we trained our model on all the data we had available, then used the model to make pre Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
dataset. A more realistic situation is when we wish to apply our model on unseen data - that is, datapoints that it did not encounter during the model fitting process.
Suppose we obtain a random sample of 6 datapoints from our population of vehicle data. We want to train a model on these 6 points and use it to make predictions on unseen data (perhaps cars for which
we don't already know the true mpg ).
[*]:
np. random. seed (100)
sample_6 = vehicles.sample(6)
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg")
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
If we design a model with polynomial degree 5, we can make perfect predictions on this sample of training data.
[*]:
degree_5_model = Pipeline( [
('polynomial_transformation', PolynomialFeatures(5)),
('linear_regression', LinearRegression( ) )
degree_5_model. fit(sample_6[["hp"] ], sample_6 ["mpg"] )
xs = np. linspace(0, 250, 1000)
degree_5_model_predictions = degree_5_model.predict(xs[ :, np.newaxis] )
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=sample_6, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50)
plt.xlim(0, 250);
However, when we reapply this fitted model to the full population of data, it fails to capture the major trends of the dataset.
[*] :
plt.plot(xs, degree_5_model_predictions, c="tab: red")
sns. scatterplot(data=vehicles, x="hp", y="mpg", s=50)
plt.ylim(-35, 50);
The model has overfit to the data used to train it. It has essentially "memorized" the six datapoints used during model fitting, and does not generalize well to new data.

children.title: One-hot encoding with scikit-learn
children.summary: The OneHotEncoder class in scikit-learn is used for one-hot encoding non-numeric data, such as days of the week. The process involves initializing the encoder, fitting it to the data to identify unique values, and then transforming the data into an array where each row has one 'hot' value and the rest are 'cold'. This method follows a similar workflow to the LinearRegression class.

children.title: Handling Unknown Categories in One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: When using one-hot encoding, it's important to drop any columns representing unknown categories, such as 'FUNDAY', which is not a valid day of the week. This situation can lead to errors during prediction if a new category appears that wasn't included in the training data, similar to encountering a new major not present in the training set.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What happens if I say? Fun day! I want to hang on to this. What's your happen? What's the one-hot and quitting a fun day? Air, why? Fun day is not a day of the week that it's all. So if I have a, a fun day column or a fun day value in that column, I should drop it. Why might this happen I might have trained on a perfectly reasonable days of the week get a set I'm going to test this. On some new customer who comes in says I'm here on FUNDAY. And as you get an error because I don't know how to include fun day. It's not unique and not a valid value. Some more important if I were trying to let's say model majors and I have like all the majors that I've seen in my training data and then Berkeley creates new major and I try to make prediction. I don't have that major when I fit the data I can't make a prediction for it. Video: 1: File Edit View Run Kernel Tabs Settings Help

children.title: Linear Regression: Data Transformation and Regularization Techniques
children.summary: Data transformation enhances linear regression by highlighting significant predictors like Fridays and Sundays in tips analysis. One-hot encoding improves interpretability, and its exclusion of the intercept term is justified by the sum of one-hot features. Regularization techniques address bias from one-hot encoding, while polynomial features allow non-linear transformations, balancing model complexity against the bias-variance tradeoff.

children.title: Linear Regression: Data Transformation and One-hot Encoding
children.summary: Data transformation integrated with the dataset enhances linear regression analysis, particularly showing the influence of weekdays on tips, with Fridays and Sundays being significant predictors. Feature engineering through Scikit Learn Pipelines improves model efficiency, and one-hot encoding aids interpretability, as demonstrated with a prediction example. The rationale for excluding the intercept term in one-hot encoded models is explained, highlighting that the sum of one-hot features equals one, and the impact of matrix rank and inversion is discussed, noting that the addition of a bias column results in a rank of 3.

children.title: Linear Regression: Data Transformation and Feature Engineering
children.summary: Data transformation can be integrated with the original dataset for linear regression, revealing the impact of weekdays on tips. Feature engineering using Scikit Learn Pipelines enhances model efficiency, with Fridays and Sundays showing greater predictive power for tips. One-hot encoding improves model interpretability, illustrated by a prediction example for a party of three with a $50 bill on a Friday, followed by a Q&A on the topic.

children.title: Linear regression: data transformation and coefficients
children.summary: Data can be transformed and joined back with the original dataset for linear regression analysis. The coefficients obtained from the linear model indicate the effect of different days of the week on the resulting tips.

children.title: Feature Engineering with Scikit Learn Pipelines
children.summary: Fridays and Sundays show higher positive predictive power for tips. Scikit Learn's Pipeline class allows for the creation of complex feature engineering pipelines that combine various transformations into a single model. This enables fitting the entire pipeline at once, streamlining the process of model creation and prediction.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What might I conclude here? Fridays and Sundays seem to have higher positive predictive power have higher effect on my tip. Good days to tip. Alright, bonus features not only, you know, future final. A psychic learn makes it easy to actually compose various feature or transformations into one giant pipeline that you can fit the entire pipeline all at once and then say predict and it does all of this stuff for you. And it manages it keeps track of it, draws cute pictures of everything that's happening. Video: 1: File Edit View Run Kernel Tabs Settings Help

You can use the scikit learn Pipeline class to define sophisticated feature engineering pipelines that combine different Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
that are all fit at once.
A pipeline is a sequence of transformations and models that are composed to form a new model. It is implemented as a list of ("name",
model/transformation) tuples. Here we have a feature engineering stage followed by a linear model. The feature engineering stage uses a
ColumnTransformer that applies named transformations to selected columns.
[10]:
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn. compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
pl = Pipeline ( [
("Feature Engineering",
ColumnTransformer(
[("OHE", OneHotEncoder(), ["day"] ),
("Polynomial", PolynomialFeatures(4), ['total_bill' ] )],
remainder="passthrough",
force_int_remainder_cols=False)),
("Linear Model", LinearRegression( )) # Linear Model
pl

children.title: One-Hot Encoding and Model Predictions
children.summary: One-hot encoding enhances model interpretability by allowing reasoning about how specific feature values influence predictions. An example was discussed where the model predicts a tip for a party of size 3 with a $50 bill on a Friday, highlighting the use of one-hot encoding for the day feature. The segment concluded with a brief Q&A on one-hot encoding.

children.title: One-hot Encoding: Excluding the Intercept Term
children.summary: The reasoning for omitting the intercept term in one-hot encoded models is discussed, emphasizing that the sum of one-hot features across rows equals one, enabling a proper linear combination without redundancy. An example illustrates how coefficients for features such as total bill and day of the week influence predictions.

children.title: One-hot Encoding: Excluding the Intercept Term
children.summary: The discussion centers on the reasoning behind not including an intercept term in one-hot encoded models. It highlights that in a well-structured model, the sum of one-hot features across rows will equal one, allowing for a proper linear combination of features without redundancy. An example is provided to illustrate how coefficients for features like total bill and day of the week impact predictions.

children.title: Matrix Rank and Inversion with One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: The addition of a bias column of ones affects the rank of a one-hot encoded matrix, resulting in a rank of 3 despite having four columns. This indicates that the matrix does not have full column rank, as the bias column can be expressed as a linear combination of the one-hot encoded columns. Consequently, the x transpose x matrix can be inverted.

children.title: Matrix Rank and One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: The addition of a column of all ones as a bias term affects the rank of the matrix formed by one-hot encoded columns. Despite having four columns, the matrix has a rank of 3 because the three one-hot encoded columns can be combined to produce the column of all ones, indicating that the matrix does not have full column rank.

children.title: Matrix Inversion and One-Hot Encoding
children.summary: The rank of the matrix is identified as 3, allowing for the inversion of the x transpose x matrix. It is emphasized that any set of one-hot encoded columns will sum to a column of all ones, and the bias column is a linear combination of the one-hot encoded columns.

children.title: Regularization, Polynomial Features, and Model Complexity
children.summary: Regularization techniques help mitigate bias in models, especially with one-hot encoding, which can cause linear dependence. Polynomial features allow for non-linear transformations to improve model fit, but increasing complexity risks underfitting or overfitting, as shown by decreasing training error. The segment also discusses the bias-variance tradeoff, emphasizing that complex models may overfit training data while performing poorly on unseen data, with a reminder for the next session on Thursday.

children.title: Regularization, Polynomial Features, and Model Complexity
children.summary: Regularization techniques address bias issues in models, particularly with one-hot encoding, where including a bias column can lead to linear dependence. Polynomial features can enhance model fit by allowing non-linear transformations, but increasing complexity can lead to underfitting or overfitting, as indicated by decreasing training error (MSE). A polynomial of degree N-1 can perfectly fit N non-overlapping data points, but this may not translate to good performance on unseen data.

children.title: Regularization and One-Hot Encoding Issues
children.summary: Regularization techniques can address issues with bias terms in models, allowing for proper mathematical treatment. When using one-hot encoding (OHE), including a bias column leads to linear dependence, making the model non-invertible. To resolve this, either omit one OHE column or avoid including an intercept term, or apply regularization.

children.title: Polynomial Features and Model Fitting
children.summary: Polynomial features can be added to a linear model to improve fit by transforming the data, such as squaring the horsepower. This process allows for non-linearities in the features while maintaining a linear combination of parameters. Continuously adding polynomial terms can enhance model performance, but the implications of this approach should be considered.

children.title: Model Complexity and Polynomial Features
children.summary: Increasing model complexity by adding polynomial features reduces training error, as indicated by decreasing Mean Squared Error (MSE) values. The trend shows a relationship between model complexity and performance, highlighting the concepts of underfitting and overfitting as complexity increases.

children.title: Model Complexity and Training Error Trends
children.summary: As model complexity increases, training error, measured by mean squared error (MSE), tends to decrease. This trend is observed in experiments with polynomial features, illustrating the concepts of underfitting and overfitting. The relationship highlights the challenge of not having access to the entire dataset in practice.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's better. The air goes down. Better fit. We kind of say his model complex increases the training area the air on the data that I have tends to go down. And that's generally been our objective up to this point. Let's minimize the air. Now we make our model richer, we can minimize the air faster. It's a deal. The real problem is that in practice we don't get to see the whole data set. We often only see a sample of the world. Right? Video: 1: Model Complexity

children.title: Perfect Polynomial Fits and Model Performance
children.summary: Given N non-overlapping data points, a polynomial of degree N-1 can perfectly fit the data, as illustrated with a degree-4 polynomial for a dataset of 5 points. However, when applying this to real data, the fit may perform poorly, as shown in a new example with a training dataset of 6 points intended for predictions on unseen data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And the cool thing about our samples is you know small set of points. Is you know we can fit the data nearly perfectly in fact with polynomials have not overlapping points. I just need a you know a degree n minus one polynomial to fit my data with zero air. I get all these funny curves depending on which date I have. Right? So I take a sample my data. And I put it back with all of my data. Here, this sample had perfect air, zero air. Great! When I went back and looked at my real data, that is a terrible looking fit. children.title: Model Performance: Overfitting and Bias-Variance Tradeoff
children.summary: A complex model may fit training data perfectly but perform poorly on unseen data, demonstrating the risk of overfitting. The variance trade-off is highlighted, showing that more complex models can lead to increased variability and sensitivity to training datasets. The bias-variance tradeoff and the importance of feature engineering will be covered in future lectures, with a reminder for the next session on Thursday.

children.title: Model Performance on Unseen Data
children.summary: A complex model can fit training data perfectly but may perform poorly on unseen data, highlighting the risk of overfitting. An example with a training dataset of 6 datapoints illustrates this issue, where a degree 5 model captures the training data well but fails to generalize.

children.title: Model Performance: Overfitting and Variance
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the generalization of models and the variance trade-off, highlighting that more complex models can lead to increased variability and potential overfitting. An example is provided where a complex degree-5 model perfectly predicts a small training dataset but fails to generalize to unseen data. The sensitivity of complex models to specific training datasets is emphasized, illustrating how they can vary significantly with different samples.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And this... Idea is generalization. And in a future lecture we'll talk about this variance trade-off that as I get. More complicated models, I get more variability in what the model could produce as a function of different samples of my data. That's parents. So as I make my models more crazy, as I add more polynomial and invent new features, it is likely my variance increases. And this puts me in this world I'm over-fitting.
